coredata.dc:title: Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Dong
Tang, Yiping
Gao, Jinwei
Chen, Ying
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: A simple, rapid and low-cost solar irradiation method to prepare highly fluorescent carbon dots has been demonstrated by using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The whole concentrated light sintering process is completed within 2 min and this nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots exhibit striking blue luminescence. Except the conventional ultra-violet (378 nm) pumped emissions, the specific up-converted photoluminescence signal is able to extend the excitation sources to near-infrared range (750 nm). Moreover, carbon dots are very sensitive and selective to the presence of Hg2+ ions. Two different responsive ways (down-conversion and up-conversion) have been employed for the first time and the limits of detection are determined to be 1.3 nM and 0.06 nM respectively. Its pH dependence has been discussed and the evolution curves indicate such carbon-based nanomaterial possess unique features within a wide pH range. This study focuses on efficient solar energy conversion and provides new insights of carbon dots with multi-modal sensing capabilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Concentrated solar light
Sensor
Luminescence
Up-conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images of the N-CDs [inset: high resolution lattice]; (b) Size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs; (b) Up-conversion emission spectra of N-CDs.
FT-IR (left) and Raman (right) spectra of N-CDs.
(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of various ions (100 nM); (b) Column graphs of N-CDs in the presence of various ions.
(a–b) TEM images of N-CDs after the addition of 10−7 M Hg2+ ion [the scale bars are 200 nm and 100 nm, respectively].
(a) Lifetime measurement of N-CDs [blank: 10.0 ns; in the presence of 10−7 M Hg2+: 9.74 ns]; (b) UV–visible spectra of N-CDs.
(a) Quenching curves of N-CDs towards various concentration of Hg2+ ion [Ex = 378 nm]; (b) linear fitting curves of (a).
(a) Quenching curves of N-CDs towards various concentration of Hg2+ ion [Ex = 750 nm]; (b) linear fitting curves of (a).
(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs within pH from 7 to 14; (b) Emission spectra of N-CDs within pH from 7 to 1; [Ex = 378 nm. Inset: normalized emission curves within pH from 7 to 1].
Schematic description of the synthesis and sensing process (average solar irradiation power 760 W/m2).
Quenching mechanism of the N-CDs for detecting Hg2+ ion in aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, rapid and low-cost solar irradiation method to prepare highly fluorescent carbon dots has been demonstrated by using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The whole concentrated light sintering process is completed within 2 min and this nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots exhibit striking blue luminescence. Except the conventional ultra-violet (378 nm) pumped emissions, the specific up-converted photoluminescence signal is able to extend the excitation sources to near-infrared range (750 nm). Moreover, carbon dots are very sensitive and selective to the presence of Hg2+ ions. Two different responsive ways (down-conversion and up-conversion) have been employed for the first time and the limits of detection are determined to be 1.3 nM and 0.06 nM respectively. Its pH dependence has been discussed and the evolution curves indicate such carbon-based nanomaterial possess unique features within a wide pH range. This study focuses on efficient solar energy conversion and provides new insights of carbon dots with multi-modal sensing capabilities.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been found as new nano-carbon members due to the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes in year 2004 [1]. They have aroused considerable interests in the past decade based on a wide variety of advantages [2]. In contrast to traditional inorganic quantum dots, carbon dots have excellent solubility, biocompatibility and facile synthesis steps, which have potential application in many fields [3–7]. Up to now, quite a few methods to synthesize carbon dots have been developed and they include hydrothermal treatment, microwave irradiation, laser ablation etc. [8] However, most of these approaches are dependent on massive electricity energy and such input irradiations will provide raw materials with heat to form carbon dots. In order to save energy in a more eco-friendly way, the search for new alternative sources will be compulsory. Solar energy is renewable and non-polluting source for high-temperature materials assembly. Since solar irradiation has already been employed for alloys modification and steel coatings [9,10], here we try to use it to generate carbon dots with its concentrated heat treatments. It has been found optical lens can harvest solar lights and raise the temperature up to hundreds of degree very shortly. This technique will offer a high temperature condition very conveniently for carbonization.

As for the surface chemistry in carbon dots, the incorporation of heteroatom especially nitrogen into the nanoplatform will change the charge distribution of carbon network and the electron transition efficiency such as quantum yields could be improved [11,12]. In this regard, citric acid and o-phenylenediamine were used as precursors. The incorporated amine groups would diminish the recombination of electron-hole pairs [13]. The high temperature achieved through the concentrated solar light irradiation has induced effective preparation of carbon dots in only 2 min. Although much progress has been performed for the synthesis of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, a series of limitations need to be solved before large-scale productions. A predominant challenge is to obtain carbon dots with broad excitation ranges. In this study, we used both down-conversion and up-conversion strategies to investigate its photophysical properties. The utilization of near-IR excitation (Ex = 750 nm) in pumping the nanomaterial was explored and blue emissions were achieved. This new findings will give up the use of ultra-violet or blue lights and the harmfulness to living systems could be avoided.

Mercury is used primarily for the manufacture of industrial chemicals or for electrical and electronic applications. However, Hg2+ is an extremely toxic substance. It will destroy human's central nervous system and kidney. There are various fluorescent sensors for the detection of mercury ions, including rare earth MOFs, aggregated induced emission frameworks, fluorescent organic molecules and inorganic quantum dots [14–17]. But the above probes generally require time-consuming synthesis steps or special sensing mediums. Moreover, the selectivity and sensitivity are still far from satisfactory. Accordingly, the design of a new responsive nano-sensor for Hg2+ will be significant. It should be mentioned that the two fluorescent ways (down-conversion and up-conversion) were employed for the first time to monitor the target ions (Hg2+) very efficiently. The detailed discussion for the microstructures, photoluminescence, absorption, stability, pH dependence and sensing process were described in following chapters.

Citric acid (CA), o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used as received. Metal nitrates, sulfates and sodium salts of anions were purchased from Aladdin and Merck. Deionized water was used for all the experiments with 18 MΩ cm. Tin paper was cut in circles (diameters = 9 cm) and were folded into funnel. Optical lens (diameters = 18 cm) was purchased in a local optical instrument company (P.R.China).

Photoluminescene (PL) spectra were measured by a Hitachi FL4600 spectrophotometer (Ex and Em slits = 10 nm) and the up-conversion spectra with excitation wavelengths between 600 and 800 nm were recorded by Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrometer. Phosphorescence measurements were determined by using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer. The delay time was set at 100 μs and scanning rate was fixed at 600 nm/min. The excitation and emission slits were both set as 10 nm. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra was measured within the 4000–500/cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer, Prestingge-21, using the KBr pellet technique. The element content was confirmed on Phenom Pro X. The microstructures were recorded by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was performed on Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern. UK). Raman spectra of the N-CDs was recorded by LabRAM HR Evolution (HORIBA FRANCE SAS), λex = 785 nm. All the experiments were operated in Guangzhou, Panyu (23°05′ N latitude and 113°37’ E longitude), China. Solar irradiation time: 12:00 a.m.-14:00 p.m., 10–20, September 2018. The sunlight irradiation power was determined by average values of six sunny days between Sept. 10th and 20th via a solar survey multifunction solar irradiance meter (seaward solar survey 200R, UK). Samples were precipitated by ethanol and dried under vacuum for X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) characterization. XRD patterns were recorded on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by using an ESCALab220I-XL spectrometer (VG company, United Kingdom).

Blue-emission CDs were prepared by the following process. Briefly, citric acid (CA, 0.1 g) was mixed with o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA, 0.05 g) [the ratio of CA:o-PDA = 2:1], then the mixture was put into a funnel-form tin paper. The experiment was carried out under direct sunlight irradiation for 2 min. The mixed solid was melted and bubbling, soon the fluid was changed from transparent to yellow brown then turned to deeper color gradually along with the release of gases. The end of the reaction was monitored by the termination of bubbled gases and the collected powders gave rise to brown-black color. The solid was dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) assisted by ultrasound irradiation. The solution was filtered three times by the membrane filter (0.20 μm), and then transferred the solution into dialysis bag (MW = 500), the dialysis of solution was lasted over 24 h to obtain the N-CDs solution. Finally, the white grey power was obtained by freeze drying for 24 h. The white grey powder (0.1 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of ultra-pure water, and then the mixture was diluted for 10 times. The obtained blue N-CDs solution (1 mg/L) was stored at 4 °C for further uses.

The quantum yield of blue CDs was calculated by using a well-accepted standard. The integrated PL intensity was plotted against absorbance for several concentrations. The gradients of the sample and reference fluorescent materials were used. Here quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution (QY 0.54 [18]), was employed to obtain the quantum yield by using the following equation:
Φ x = Φ s
(
A r
A x
)
(
η
x
2
η
s
2
)
(
D
x
D
s
)

In equation, the subscripts “x” and “s” refer to sample and standard.“Φ” is the fluorescence quantum yield,“A” is the absorbance, “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. “D” is fluorescence peak area. The absorbance should be below 0.05 in order to avoid inner filter effect.

Formation process will affect the microstructure, property and persistence of engineered carbon-based materials in reactive systems. Considering the preparation routes to assemble carbon dots, most of them require long reaction hours or high-temperature-processing conditions. Concentrated solar irradiation, a clean and eco-friendly technology, will assist the carbon nanomaterial synthesis in high efficiency. As presented in Scheme 1 , the achieved blue N-CDs were constructed by concentrating solar light irradiation on the mixture of CA and o-PDA which were dispersed at the bottom of funnel shaped tin paper. The energy required for chemical conversions via solar lights can be carried out within only 2 min and the crude product of N-CDs would be obtained through purification process.

In Fig. 1 a, the HRTEM image of N-CDs showed that the particles were dispersed well with the lattice spacing of 0.314 nm (Fig. 1a inset and Fig. S1), which were matched well with <002> spacing of graphitic carbon. In XRD analysis, a broad band was observed at around 24° and the signal was attributed to the <002> lattice spacing of graphitic-like structure (Fig. S2). The nanocrystals were in the range of 3–5 nm with an average size of 4.31 nm (Fig. 1b). Its chemical composition was further clarified via XPS studies (Fig. S3). Three resolved peaks in C1s spectrum were observed at 284.4, 285.6 and 287.8 eV, which were assigned to CC/CC, CN and CN/CO groups. The N1s spectrum included two peaks at 399 and 400.2 eV, which were corresponding to amino N and pyrrolic N. In O1s spectrum, the results revealed the presence of two bands at 531.6 and 532.9 eV which were ascribed to CO and CO units.

Photoluminescence spectra were given in Fig. 2 a, the measured excitation wavelengths were located at 257 and 378 nm. The broad band at 462 nm was the maximum emission signal. It has to be mentioned that the quantum yield of the carbon dots has been determined to be 65% at room temperature within ambient atmosphere. Compared with reported literature, this efficiency value is relatively high and favorable for future applications (Table S1) [19–25]. We also measured 3D photoluminescence scan spectra of N-CDs, the emission peak maintained relatively stable at 462 nm under different excitation wavelengths (Figs. S4 and S5). Such excitation-independent feature might be related to uniform energy levels of optical centers in the carbon internal structure or surfaces. In general, phosphorescence effect would be less common than fluorescence, since it involved triplet states. In this research, the room temperature phosphorescence band at 470 nm has been observed in the absence of heavy atoms in carbon dots (Fig. S6). We can understand that fluorescence signals were collected via the electron transitions from singlet state to the ground state and the emission curves were measured synchronously with the excitation pulses. However, phosphorescence was caused by transitions via triplet states through intersystem crossing from singlet to triplet and its signal was determined by time delay. In other words, when incident irradiation was terminated, the optical signal could persist for some time at room temperature. In addition, the energy level of such spin-forbidden pathway was generally slightly lower than fluorescence.

The principle of up-conversion refers to the excitation radiation would occur in multiphoton active process. Normally this effect was closely related with lanthanide containing phosphors or two-photon absorption molecules [26]. Fig. 2b demonstrated that the as-prepared N-CDs possessed up-conversion character. The selection of excitation wavelengths between 600 and 800 nm (interval = 50 nm) could sensitize the N-CDs and the best excitation wavelength was located at 750 nm. It is worthy to say that the upconversion is considered to be a process including sequential absorptions of more photons and emits at short wavelength upon excitation in the longer wavelength. The special anti-Stokes emission feature of carbon dots would be caused by quantum confinement effect and surface functional groups. As far as the surface modification is concerned, the encapsulation of heteroatom (nitrogen) into carbon lattice would alter the orientation of electron networks. Nitrogen possesses greater electronegativity and rich valence electrons. Such characteristic might lead to dissimilarity in the energy levels of various electron states which would play main roles in jumping between equilibrium and excited states. In this way, the multiphoton active process which is closely related to up-conversion could be realized. Additionally, such carbon nanostructure contained unsaturated bonds and would occur ground-state multiplicity (π and σ orbitals) [27]. π electrons might transition to a high energy state (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) and turn back to the lower energy state (σ orbital). Thus upconverted photoluminescence was achieved.

To gain further insights into the chemical structure of the carbon dots, FTIR spectra supported the presence of multiple functional groups (Fig. 3 a). The band at 3511 cm−1 would be caused by the vibrations of oxygen-containing groups (OH), and the vibrations of NH were situated in 3440 and 3374 cm−1. The peak at 2926 cm−1 was ascribed to alkyl CH stretching vibration. The curve showed amide signals at 1638 cm−1, affirming the existence of amidation reaction. N-CDs displayed CN stretching (1385 cm−1) vibrations, indicating amide linkages might be formed during the oxidation process under sunlight irradiation. The characteristic peaks at 1270 and 1140 cm−1 were ascribed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C O C. In addition, CH bending vibration was observed and the signal was assigned to the peak at 856 cm−1. Raman analysis was provided in Fig. 3b. D and G bands of N-CDs, which were indicative to the structural information of carbon materials, were observed at ∼1402 cm−1 and ∼1638 cm−1, respectively. The intensity of D and G bands were the presence of defective carbon moieties and well-organized carbon structure with in-plane vibrations sp2 clusters. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.862, illustrating that the surface defects would be present and oxygenated species existed during the carbonization procedure.

As the functional fluorescent materials, carbon dots could be employed for sensitive detection in various targets [28–33]. The selectivity of the probe for Hg2+ ion was carefully examined among various relevant metal ions including K+, Na+, NH4 +, Ag+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Bi3+ (Fig. 4 a). It was able to see that the addition of 100 nM other metal ions could hardly affect the emission intensity at 462 nm. Nevertheless, the same concentration of Hg2+ ion would enormously decrease the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4b). The corresponding photographs were displayed in Fig. S7, only Hg2+ ion can effectively switch off the blue luminescence.

With the aim of investigating the evolution process more clearly, HRTEM images in the presence of Hg2+ ion (100 nM) were shown in Fig. 5 a–b. The morphology of N-CDs due to the addition of Hg2+ ion was completely different from original carbon dots and the size of aggregates would nearly reach several hundred nanometers. The results firmly supported that Hg2+ ion could induce the particle growth or agglomerations and the drastic structure change might lead to the emission quenching. The zeta potential of N-CDs increased from 3.04 to 25.3 mV after the addition of 100 nM Hg2+ ion into N-CDs solution (Fig. S8). EDS spectrum was collected and Hg was recorded (Fig. S8 and Table S2). It also supported e existence of C, N and O elements of carbon dots. In addition, the elemental mapping images also indicated the distribution of such elements (Fig. S9). In order to further study the composition, the aggregated particles in the presence of Hg2+ were collected and we carried out XRD measurement (Fig. S10). The curve was similar to the original carbon dots with the difference that the intensity was suppressed. The broad diffraction band again suggested the presence of organic segments and revealed the existence of carbon dots.

In this case, the well-accepted concentration quenching effect induced the “on-off” changes and the close interactions among the fluorophores in the excited and ground states resulted in the fast growth of nanoparticles as shown in Fig. 5a. Scheme 2 described the chemical process and the detection mechanism. We have also compared the lifetimes of N-CDs before and after the addition of Hg2+ ion. The results showed that lifetime had no substantial changes (Fig. 6 a). It is well documented that fluorescence quenching has been regarded as the process to decrease emission intensity and it has been generally divided into two pathways including dynamic and static quenching [34]. The former quenching is a time-dependent process and the quencher would diffuse to the fluorophore during the excited states. The static quenching refers to the interactions will be formed in the ground states. The determination of lifetimes could be the most reliable way to distinguish dynamic (lifetime change) and static quenching (lifetime almost constant) [35]. Another way to ascertain the two forms would be the evaluation of the absorption spectra [36]. The ground state interactions would lead to perturbation of the absorption spectra of fluorophore in static quenching and further evidence was provided in Fig. 6b. UV–visible spectra of N-CDs included two typical absorption bands at 251 and 271 nm and the signals are derived from π-electron transitions. The peak at short wavelength (251 nm) was corresponding to π-π* transitions of CC unit. The band at 271 nm was attributed to n-π* transition of CO segment. A tail band (368 nm) extending to nearly visible range can be assigned to the n-π* transition from surface domains. After the addition of 100 nM Hg2+ ion into the N-CDs aqueous solution, the absorption peak at 368 nm was reduced significantly. The broaden signal verified the perturbation of the absorption spectra and the effective binding through Hg2+ ions supported the reliability of a static quenching process.

The N-CDs emission signals of in the presence of Hg2+ ion were provided in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 , (λex = 378 nm for Figs. 7 and 750 nm for Fig. 8). Fig. 7 recorded the fluorescent spectra upon the addition of Hg2+ ion gradually (λex = 378 nm) and the emission intensity decreased in the concentration range between 0 and 150 nM. The relation between (F0 F)/F0 and concentration of Hg2+ ion demonstrated a good linear relation (Fig. 7b), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.3 nM (from 0 to 20 nM). It is known that up-conversion luminescence with longer excitation wavelengths possessed a variety of merits compared with down-conversion emissions. Its penetration depth, low damage to the surface would generate extensive interests in laser update, optical storage and medical diagnosis. Herein, when the excitation wavelength was replaced by 750 nm, the correlation between (F0 F)/F0 and concentration of Hg2+ ion was also followed by a linear function. The up-conversion signal at 447 nm was gradually suppressed in the range from 0 to 10 nM, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.06 nM (Fig. 8b). The limit of detection value in current study has been compared with numerous published results and the comparison profiles were given in Table S3 [37–51]. In the obtained chart, the detection limit of this carbon dots was not the lowest one. It still gave rise to the acceptable sensitivity as compared with the other methods. More importantly, it demonstrated the following advantages. At first, it possesses simplicity of materials preparation within just one step. Particularly, the effective design cost only 2 min upon the green energy source (solar light) irradiations and this treatment significantly reduces reaction time and will be energy-saving. Secondly, the achieved quantum yield is very high and such quantum dots will be valuable for the future uses in biological and environmental fields. More importantly, it is appealing to obtain a multi-mode probe for the detection of Hg2+. In this contribution, the optical probe with dual-response (up-conversion and down-conversion) potentials has been certified.

Generally, the optical sensor would be applied in different environments and we explored its fluorescence responses to pH values. From pH = 7 to pH = 14 (Fig. 9 a), the blue luminescence was enhanced step by step, suggesting that the strong alkaline condition could effectively suppress the non-irradiative transitions and save the emission efficiency. At acidic environment, its fluorescence was gradually reduced and the emission wavelength shifted to 493 nm. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CDs showed promising uses in pH sensing field (Fig. 9). It is accepted that the surface passivation and modification will be the decisive factor to achieve the photoluminescence of carbon dots [52]. The increased acidity will provide more protons to amino groups. The nitrogen atom binding with H+ would induce redistribution of the electrons and the intramolecular charge transfer effect might be reinforced. Accordingly, a lower energy in excited states of emissive species would be obtained. As for the stability, Fig. S11 certified that the N-CDs still remained 95% of its original fluorescence intensity after continuous measurements for 30 days.

We reported an efficient green strategy for the synthesis of carbon dots through concentrated solar light irradiations. The chemical reaction could be performed in 2 min and blue emission was obtained. A unique feature of the as-derived carbon dots would be its excitation-independent behavior and such blue band could be evolved within a wide range of pH environments. Its luminescence property can be tuned by selecting different excitation wavelengths and up-conversion effect was achieved. The promising uses of the carbon nanomaterial would be the employment of such nanoplatform as a functional sensor to detect Hg2+. To our knowledge, it will be the first time that the optical probe revealed high sensitivity with ultra-low limits of detection via both down-conversion and up-conversion modes. Overall, the current results support this zero-dimensional carbon dots could be developed as novel responsive systems for trace element detections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China-Guangdong Joint funding support (No. U1801256) and Innovative team project by Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). Q. M. appreciates National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2016A050502053). D. Lu is grateful to the experimental assistance provided by Dr. Zhiyong Yang and Dr. Yingxiao Mu.
